preamble: The shift from a voiced velar stop [g] to a voiced palatal stop [ɟ] is a standard palatalization process triggered by front vowels or the palatal glide [j]. This stage, often as an intermediate step toward affrication (to [dʒ] or [dz]), is a hallmark of the Slavic First Palatalization and early Romance language developments. Hungarian maintains [ɟ] (written 'gy') as a separate phoneme.

examples.note: Hungarian has a phonemic voiced palatal stop /ɟ/ (written 'gy'), historically often from palatalized velars or dentals.
Initial stage of the First Slavic Palatalization of /g/ before shifting to [dʒ] (Slavic [ž] in this context).
Pre-affrication stage of Latin /g/ before front vowels.
Latin /g/ palatalized to [ɟ] before front vowels, a precursor to Italian [dʒ].

sources: Ladefoged, P., & Maddieson, I. (1996). The Sounds of the World's Languages.
Schenker, A. M. (1995). The Dawn of Slavic.
Alkire, T., & Rosen, C. (2010). Romance Philology.
